entry.title: LMS Organization Connector File - All Levels
entry.content:
Hello,

Does anyone have an integration center job created for the Organization Connector, that includes multiple levels (I.E., Legal Entity, Business Unit, Department, Division) and does not cause duplication exceptions during every run?

The goal is to have ONE Org file, that gets loaded into LMS on a recurring basis, but that includes the hierarchy and not only the one lowest level of the organization. This is because having all levels provides more flexibility when using Assignment Profiles - for example by being able to select a Business Unit and simply using the 'Include Sub Entities Of' operator to also gather all departments and divisions underneath it.

As of right now, we use multiple different integration center jobs for each level, but this is not the best solution as multiple files have to be accounted for/managed.

Any insight on the Organization Connector input file is greatly appreciated! 

Thank you

Melanie

There is an issue with the order in which the comments are displayed for most of the old questions/posts on this platform. 

Comments are displayed  in a haphazard order and don't seem to follow a pattern like chronological. There is also no option given to us to sort the comments (chronologically or by kudos, etc.). This makes it difficult for anyone to make sense of the comments posted on let's say 1st Nov 2015 because all the comments show the same date and no time-stamp. The more the number of comments more confusing it is for us.

For instance, take this Question/Post from 2012 which has just 4 comments. That post is displayed to me as below (screenshot attached), where the comments are not chronologically sorted. Now imagine the problem if there are more than 10 comments.

This problem has been there in the old design too. Not sure if has to do with my account or region settings or if it is a actual intended functionality of this platform. I hope it gets resolved soon.

Thanks,

entry.title: Manage Price - Sales : Sales and distribution fields not filled in result grid
entry.content:
Hi,

Using Manage Price - Sales, our people want to be able to see the fields Sector, Sub-Sector, Product Family, Distribution and Material Group 5.

I can't seem to add image in my post, but in summary we do have values for those fields in "Display Material" for our distribution channel.

When we use the button settings in  "Manage Price - Sales", we can add the columns Sector, Sub-Sector, Product Family, Distribution and Material Group 5 but no data appear in those columns.

From what i saw on another post, this application is using OData API SD_PRICING_CONDITIONRECORD_SRV service. Internally for the app, SAP makes use of SD_PRICING_CONDITIONRECORD_SRV, which in turn calls on C_SLSPRICINGCONDITIONRECORDTP, a local Business Object Interface which retrieves the right data.

So i checked the view C_SLSPRICINGCONDITIONRECORDTP and its children and effectively i didn't find the missing fields anywhere in the view or its child views...

So my question is:

Are those fields really not part of the result data for "Manage Price - Sales" ?

If so, why can we choose those fields in "settings" of the grid ?

Is there a note to show those fields ?

If not, is it possible to create a ZC_SLSPRICINGCONDITIONRECORDTP ? If so, is it a big development ?

Thanks!

PS. In "Manage Price - Sales", the status of the Condition Record is "Released" and i gave myself the "SAP_ALL" permission in our QA environment just in case it was a permission problem.

entry.title: Re: CJIA report payment issue
entry.content:
Hi Volodymyr!

This report simply lists all individual line items. The responsibility for evaluating and analyzing the listed data lies with the user. It is up to the user to assess which items are still unpaid, which have already been requested, and which have been settled.

The purpose of the report is solely to provide a complete list of entries, allowing the user to carry out their own analysis based on their specific needs.

Regards
Marcio Blos

entry.title: Re: Text Field with more than characters
entry.content:
Dear @user2001 ,

First of all,  since you have created UDF as Alphanumeric-Regular, its limitation is 254 Char, you need to create UDF as Alphanumeric-Text which allows 2 GB of text in header fields and 255 KB of text in row fields.

Usually T&C are applicable for the entire document. Right click the marketing documents, you will have option for opening and closing remarks. That should be enough for the T&C.

If the T&C are are fixed text, then design them in the CR Layout to avoid users changing them in the document.

If you have requirement of T&C to be used per row item, then follow @Maya_Shiff 's suggestion.

Thanks.

entry.title: My SAP Sapphire Orlando experience
entry.content:
Hello, I'm Brian Kyser, a solution advisor with over 20 years of experience in the technology sector. I work with customers to help understand their issues and map those issues back to the value that SAP can provide. I like to work with customers to help them shape their vision of their respective programs to attain the most value out of an SAP framework. I aim to share my insights on how SAP's comprehensive service solutions can optimize your service processes, increase customer satisfaction, and achieve operational efficiency. 

This year I was fortunate to speak at SAP Sapphire Orlando. My session “Revolutionize customer service with AI agents in SAP Service Cloud” was about  how to connect the SAP Service Cloud solution with the SAP ERP application to optimize data, workforce, and processes while leveraging AI at every stage from issue to resolution to boost productivity and outcomes. Discover how SAP Service Cloud automates customer service processes and accelerates resolutions, driving efficiency with AI agents.  

The goal for the presentation was to show a day in the life journey from customer interaction all the way to field service work being assigned and the fact that this could be achieved through one application. Below were the main topics I covered: 

SAP Service Cloud manages customer service from interaction to resolution. Key features include: 

  1. Omnichannel Communication: Meet customers on their preferred channels. 
  2. Agent Desktop: Handle inquiries efficiently from any channel. 
  3. Case Management: Resolve customer cases efficiently. 
  4. Collaboration: Enable teamwork within and outside customer service.      
  5. Service Order Creation: Ensure end-to-end resolution with native service orders.      
  6. Case Intelligence: Resolve issues faster with intelligent assistance.      
  7. Topic Analyzer: Identify trends in cases for improvements.                   
  8. Feedback Gathering: Gain insights to improve based on customer feedback.          
  9. Field service technician scheduled directly from Service Cloud  

In addition to the end to end journey, I also highlighted our AI Agents powered by Joule for CX:  

Case Classification Agent 

Purpose: Categorize and prioritize new tickets without historical data. Tools: Read case details, apply business rules, and update fields. 

Q&A Agent 

Purpose: Answer product-related questions in service cases. Tools: Identify questions, search product documentation, and suggest answers. 

Knowledge Creation Agent 

Purpose: Generate knowledge articles from closed cases. Tools: Analyze closed cases, extract key information, and create articles. 

Conclusion 

SAP optimizes service processes, enhancing customer satisfaction and operational efficiency. SAP Service Cloud, SAP S/4HANA Cloud, and SAP Field Service Management work together to streamline operations, ensuring teams can focus on customer outcomes. 

Intelligent agents like the Case Classification Agent, Q&A Agent, and Knowledge Creation Agent further enhance operations. 

Before ending this post, I would like to share with you my personal experience from SAP Sapphire Orlando. 

My SAP Sapphire session was a high-octane sprint! I quickly iterated through key slides before diving deep into a dynamic demo. We kicked off with AWS Connect, seamlessly orchestrating a customer handoff to a live agent, showcasing a robust Service Cloud-to-S/4HANA-to-Field Service Management pipeline. The entire 20-minute journey, Q&A included, was supercharged with AI, demonstrating its pervasive enhancement capabilities. It was truly validating to connect with customers who are not just excited, but actively seeking to integrate our cutting-edge generative AI tech into their operations ASAP. 

SAP Sapphire consistently delivers. It's an unparalleled nexus for engaging with diverse industry players, all converging on shared objectives yet navigating unique tech stacks to get there. My prime takeaway? AI and integration aren't just buzzwords; they're mission-critical imperatives for most enterprises. However, their true transformative power is only unleashed when underpinned by resilient, high-performance tech foundations. It's about optimizing for maximum value extraction in this rapidly evolving digital landscape. 

Now Let’s Hear from You 

Before ending the blog post, I want to share that one of the things I enjoyed the most at SAP Sapphire was meeting new people. Who said this kind of fun needs to end at SAP Sapphire? 🙂 

Please introduce yourself in the comment section and share what you do in the world of SAP. Also, I would like to invite @BrendanDelBiondo  to introduce himself. 

That’s me with the vest!

entry.title: BiWhy – OS Monitoring. Cloud Infrastructure Pitfalls.
entry.content:
Issue:

BOBJ services failed to start after a routine system reboot, despite no configuration changes.

 

Initial Findings:

Process Monitor and BiWhy identified SPLUNK (an auxiliary OS monitoring tool) as a lead CPU consumer. Disabling SPLUNK, however, had no impact.

 

Root Cause & Resolution:

A quick review of hardware metrics in BiWhy and comparison witha healthy node, revealed unusually high disk queue lengths and utilization on the affected node.

 

Disk performance tests confirmed the disk was operating up to 40x slower than normal.

The issue was brought to the Cloud/VM team, who replaced the disk. Services resumed normal operation.

Apparently some hardware change event was triggered by cloud infrastructure software, with no notification to the tenant.

 

Takeaway:

It’s unclear why enterprise-grade tools like SPLUNK failed to detect the root cause.

Whether due to software limitations, complex configuration, or unintuitive interfaces that hinder effective use by administrators, but this is not the first time a simple solution like BiWhy has identified an issue where enterprise tools have not.

A simple, intuitive, and informative UI is often what enterprise software lacks.

 

Screenshots:

entry.title: Re: Purchasing tax for Canada import
entry.content:
Hi Lucie,

To make U.S. imports non-taxable in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, follow these steps

  • Create a tax code (e.g. I0) with 0% rate for import purchases.

In “Manage Tax Condition Records”, maintain entries for

  • Ship-to region (ON/QC) + Vendor country (US) → assign the I0 tax code.

Please make sure the product tax classification supports exemption if it's part of your condition setup.

This ensures Canadian taxes don’t apply to U.S. suppliers even though the ship-to is in Ontario or Quebec.

SAP Help References 

Tax Determination in Purchasing – S4HC

SSCUI 100297 – Define Tax Codes for Sales and Purchases

Let me know if you're using any third-party tax service (like Vertex or Avalara), as the logic may differ.

With kind regards

Chuma

.

entry.title: Re: SAPUI5 in WinGUI (dialog popup, sap.m etc)
entry.content:
Slipstream works only in a browser, so it will not help with SAP GUI for Windows.

As for using SAPUI5 in the Windows GUI: I have no experience with that, but this is what AI says about the topic:

>>>>>
Yes, it is technically possible to embed or use SAPUI5 within SAP GUI for Windows, though it's not the primary or recommended way SAP intends for UI5 applications to be consumed. SAPUI5 is fundamentally a web-based (HTML5) UI technology, while SAP GUI is a desktop application.

The most common method to achieve this integration is by using the HTML Control (or Web Viewer Control) within a SAP GUI Dynpro screen.

Here's how it generally works and the considerations:

How to Embed SAPUI5 in SAP GUI (Conceptual Steps):

  1. Develop Your SAPUI5 Application:

    • Create your SAPUI5 application as a standalone web application. This could be hosted on an ABAP front-end server (as a BSP application or via SAP Gateway/Fiori Launchpad infrastructure), on a separate web server, or even on SAP BTP (Business Technology Platform).
    • Ensure your SAPUI5 app is designed to be embedded and can handle being run within an iframe. This might involve setting frameOptions: 'allow' in your index.html or ensuring appropriate X-Frame-Options and Content-Security-Policy headers are configured on the server hosting the UI5 app.

  2. Create an ABAP Dynpro Program:

    • In SAP GUI (using SE80 or SE38), create an ABAP program with a Dynpro screen (e.g., screen number 9000).
    • On this Dynpro screen, add a Custom Control. This is usually a CL_GUI_CUSTOM_CONTAINER object. Give it a meaningful name (e.g., CUSTOM_CONTAINER_UI5).
    • Set the resize flags (vertically and horizontally) for the custom container in the Dynpro layout to ensure the embedded content scales correctly.

  3. Instantiate and Use the HTML Control:

    • In the ABAP program's PBO (Process Before Output) module for the Dynpro, you will instantiate the CL_GUI_CUSTOM_CONTAINER and then an CL_GUI_HTML_VIEWER (or CL_GUI_BROWSER_CONTROL for older versions).
    • The CL_GUI_HTML_VIEWER object takes the custom container as its parent.
    • Crucially, you will call the SHOW_URL method (or similar) of the HTML viewer control, passing the URL of your deployed SAPUI5 application.
       

ABAP Code Snippet Example (Simplified PBO Module):

ABAP
 
MODULE PBO_9000 OUTPUT.

  DATA: lo_custom_container TYPE REF TO cl_gui_custom_container,
        lo_html_viewer      TYPE REF TO cl_gui_html_viewer.

  IF lo_custom_container IS INITIAL.
    " Create custom container for the HTML viewer
    CREATE OBJECT lo_custom_container
      EXPORTING
        container_name = 'CUSTOM_CONTAINER_UI5' " Name of the custom control on your Dynpro
      EXCEPTIONS
        OTHERS         = 1.
    IF sy-subrc <> 0.
      MESSAGE 'Error creating custom container' TYPE 'E'.
      RETURN.
    ENDIF.

    " Create HTML Viewer (Web Browser Control)
    CREATE OBJECT lo_html_viewer
      EXPORTING
        parent = lo_custom_container
      EXCEPTIONS
        OTHERS = 1.
    IF sy-subrc <> 0.
      MESSAGE 'Error creating HTML viewer' TYPE 'E'.
      RETURN.
    ENDIF.

    " Define the URL of your SAPUI5 application
    " This URL would point to your Fiori Launchpad app, BSP application, or external web server
    DATA(lv_ui5_app_url) = 'https://<your_fiori_launchpad_host>:<port>/sap/bc/ui5_ui5/sap/<your_ui5_app_name>/index.html'.

    " Load the SAPUI5 application into the HTML viewer
    CALL METHOD lo_html_viewer->show_url
      EXPORTING
        url    = lv_ui5_app_url
      EXCEPTIONS
        OTHERS = 1.
    IF sy-subrc <> 0.
      MESSAGE 'Error showing UI5 URL' TYPE 'E'.
    ENDIF.

  ENDIF.

ENDMODULE.

Key Considerations and Limitations:

  • User Experience: While technically possible, this approach creates a "browser in a window" experience within SAP GUI. It might not feel as seamless or integrated as a native Fiori Launchpad experience.
  • Context Passing: Passing data or context between the traditional SAP GUI Dynpro and the embedded SAPUI5 application can be challenging. You'd typically need to embed parameters in the URL for initial loading, and for real-time interaction, you might have to resort to more complex mechanisms like Web Sockets or polling, which adds complexity.
  • Look and Feel: The SAPUI5 application will render using its own theming (e.g., Belize, Quartz), which might clash visually with the classic SAP GUI theme.
  • Browser Engine: The HTML Control in SAP GUI for Windows relies on the Internet Explorer engine (or the new Chromium-based WebView2 control in newer SAP GUI versions - 7.70+ with specific patches). Ensure compatibility with the browser engine used. Edge (Chromium-based) is generally more modern and supports more web standards than the legacy IE engine.
  • Security: Be mindful of cross-origin policies (X-Frame-Options, Content-Security-Policy). The server hosting your SAPUI5 application must allow it to be embedded in an iframe on the SAP GUI client's domain.

In summary, while you can technically embed SAPUI5 in SAP GUI, it's generally done for specific, niche requirements rather than as a broad strategy. For modernization and a consistent user experience, SAP's roadmap points towards SAP Fiori and the Fiori Launchpad.
<<<<<

Based on this, it looks like Personas will not help, although I could imagine trying to add an HTML Viewer control to your flavor and follow the method described above. However, I think that even if you get it working somehow, it will probably be rather cumbersome, and it doesn't seem it's worth the effort.

entry.title: Recap: SAP Sapphire Orlando 2025: Transform your business with SAP BTP Use Cases
entry.content:
Hello, SAP community!

I'm Joanne Koh-Stewart, Director of Product Marketing for SAP BTP for Business Applications and Core. I spend my days identifying SAP BTP use cases that solve real customer challenges across your Business Suite and lines of business. At Sapphire 2025, I had the privilege of connecting with hundreds of SAP customers at different sessions and at the booth.

As a follow-up to my pre-SAP Sapphire blog post, "Explore business scenarios enabled by SAP BTP that you can implement today," I excited to share some of the main highlights, insights, and key takeaways from the sessions.

 

 

Session 1: Explore business scenarios enabled by SAP BTP that you can implement today

You've heard the SAP Business Technology Platform success stories. But where do you actually start? How do you move from "this sounds promising" to "this is transforming our business"?

During my session "Explore business scenarios enabled by SAP BTP that you can implement today," we showcased four new SAP BTP use cases added to the SAP BTP use cases that are already transforming how organizations operate. These aren't future concepts – they're available today, with step-by-step implementation guides. Think of these use cases as your GPS navigation for business transformation:

  • They show you the fastest route to your business objectives
  • They help you avoid common implementation roadblocks
  • They provide step-by-step guidance to navigate to your destination

My colleague @denisema  also highlighted something you won't want to miss: the free tier model that allows your organization to test-drive powerful SAP BTP services at zero cost before making any commitment.

Get the full breakdown of the four new use cases in my companion article Connect, Automate, and Thrive with 4 SAP BTP Use Cases You Cannot Miss at Sapphire 2025

 

Session 2: Mission possible: Accelerate transformation with SAP Discovery Center

In my second session, "Mission possible: Accelerate transformation with SAP Discovery Center," we moved beyond theory into practice. I had the privilege of being joined by the two customer panellists:

  • Dr. Ronald Freeze from the University of Arkansas
  • Alex Burkel from Border States Industries

These are real organizations that used SAP Discovery Center to drive innovation and deliver practical outcomes. They discovered Missions built specifically to address business problems, learned to navigate SAP BTP with unprecedented speed and flexibility, and most importantly, used these tools to solve their existing business challenges. We discussed how they 'discovered' SAP Discovery Center, which Missions they utilized for their organizations, as well as their learnings and best practices

 

Recommended resources to continue your learning journey:

To take your business to the next level with SAP Business Technology Platform Use Cases: Here are some resources available for you.

  • SAP Business Technology Platform Use Cases – One Pager
  • SAP Business Technology Platform Use Case Page
  • SAP Discovery Center
  • SAP Business Accelerator Hub
  • SAP Learning

Related community managed tags you should subscribe to remain informed about this topic: 

  • SAP Business Technology Platform
  • SAP Build
  • SAP Business Suite
  • SAP Integration Suite

Related community topic pages: 

  •  SAP Business Technology Platform In Action

 

SAP Sapphire for me:

What truly energized me at Sapphire was conversations with customers who asked focused questions about implementing SAP BTP within their specific business context. Their enthusiasm confirmed that transformation happens when practical solutions meet accessible entry points. 

Whether you're looking to connect disparate systems, automate complex processes, or create a new business application, your SAP BTP journey can begin today. The question is not whether SAP BTP can transform your organization – it's how quickly you can get started. The map is ready. Where will you go first?

Ready to start your own success story with SAP BTP? I invite you to turn your business challenges into success stories at next year's Sapphire by:

  1. Exploring the existing SAP BTP use cases 
  2. Testing capabilities through the free tier model
  3. Walking through the step-by-step guidance on SAP Discovery Center
  4. Connecting with me directly to discuss your specific challenges

  

Now let’s hear from you:  

Before ending the blog post, I want to share that one of the things I enjoyed the most in SAP Sapphire was meeting new people. Who said this kind of fun needs to end in SAP Sapphire?:) Please introduce yourself in the comments section and share what you do in the world of SAP. Also, I would like to invite @denisema  to introduce herself.

entry.title: Building a Compelling Business Case to Move Your Database to the Cloud
entry.content:
Migrating to SAP HANA Cloud can be a game-changer for your business, offering unparalleled performance, scalability, and cost efficiency. By building a strong business case, you can clearly articulate the benefits and ROI to stakeholders, making it easier to gain their support and approval. Embrace the future of data management and take your business to the next level with SAP HANA Cloud. This blog will guide you through building a compelling business case to move your database to SAP HANA Cloud. 

To start, focus on an analysis of your current environment.  Describe your on-premise data infrastructure, including hardware, software, and maintenance requirements as well as the costs associated with maintenance, licensing, and IT staff.  Also, highlight any challenges you are currently facing, such as limited scalability, performance issues, or high downtime.

Now, it is time to detail out the benefits of moving to SAP HANA Cloud.  We can help!  Together with IDC, we have developed an online tool - a short assessment to help you communicate the potential value of using HANA Cloud.

The assessment is just 6 questions and will generate a personalized report to include potential cost savings, performance and productivity improvements, business enablement benefits, and return on investment.

Next, conduct a comprehensive TCO comparison between your current on-premises setup and the proposed HANA Cloud solution. Make sure to factor in both the upfront and ongoing costs for each scenario.  Based on the projected cost savings and business benefits, estimate the expected ROI of the cloud migration (the personalized report will help you here). This helps stakeholders understand the long-term value of the proposed project.

Finally, think further about your migration strategy, including any tools and services you'll use to simplify the process.  We can also assist you here!  Learn about a wide a range of migration automation tools, accelerators, and services to streamline the complete migration journey to SAP HANA Cloud - from initial planning to go-live, including a free migration assessment to help you design an optimal architecture and deployment strategy.  

At this point, you will also want to address potential risks, like data security concerns or migration downtime, and propose strategies to mitigate them. This shows that you've thoroughly considered all aspects of the migration. Then. summarize your findings and make a clear, data-driven recommendation on whether to proceed with the cloud migration.  Outline potential the next steps for the migration project, including planning, proof of concept (PoC), execution, and ongoing monitoring.

Good luck in taking the first step towards database modernization!

Learn from IDC’s business value study on SAP HANA Cloud.

entry.title: Re: Did you know this about Capacity Planning in S/4HANA?
entry.content:
Hello Jenn,

I found this blog very helpful; thank you! 

I just want to confirm that the Capacity Scheduling Table cannot do these 3 things that I am used to doing using CM21/CM25:

1. Select several operations and sequence/sort them when dispatching using a configured dispatch sequence. For example, sort first by Setup Group and secondly by Setup Group Key and thirdly by Material number.

2. Use a Setup Matrix to replace the operations' setup time with a time from the Setup Matrix when dispatching several operations at once.

3. Left justify operations so that when the work center is at only, say, 80% loaded the operations will dispatch to the left, leaving, say, all day Friday open with no scheduled work.

Please confirm.  Thank you and best regards, Ann

entry.title: Why are the customer invoices not listed in the Open Item Management for G/L Accounts?
entry.content:
Dear All,

Why are the customer invoices not listed in the Open Item Management for G/L Accounts?

Regards.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Why are the customer invoices not listed in the Open...
entry.content:
Dear All,

As line items from customer invoices or credit memos are always posted to the Sales subledger, you cannot use open item management for such line items, regardless of whether you have selected the Open Item Management checkbox.

Refer to the below documentation for additional details, please:

KBA 1786985 - Transactions Not Listed in Open Item Management for G/L Accounts https://me.sap.com/notes/1786985

Open Item Management and G/L Accounts: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_BUSINESS_BYDESIGN/2754875d2d2a403f95e58a41a9c7d6de/2c215121722d10149393937d82ccfece.html?locale=en-US

Regards,
Anderson
SAP Support

entry.title: How to set build parameters in package.json for dif envs without maintaining separate branches?​
entry.content:
Context​​:
Current package.json has hardcoded build commands:

"build:ssr": "yarn build:prod && ng run ofs-spartacus:server:production"

This requires manual changes for each environment (e.g., prod → dev/stage), leading to branch proliferation.

since this file package.json is part of git version control every time when we need to deploy code to dev we have to make changes in above line and need to change prod to dev or in case if staging deployment is needed then prod to stage. 

 

Can these values be dynamically set ? so that when deployed based on the environment appropriate build ssr is triggered ?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: How to set build parameters in package.json for dif e...
entry.content:
1. ​​Modify package.json​​ to use environment variables:
    "scripts": { "build:ssr": "yarn build:${BUILD_ENV1} && ng run ofs-spartacus:server:${BUILD_ENV2}" }
  2. ​​Set variables at runtime​​:
    export BUILD_ENV1=dev # or 'stage'/'prod'
    export BUILD_ENV2=development # or 'staging'/'production'
    yarn build:ssr
  3. ​​Validation​​: Test variable substitution works across environments.

​​Note​​: Requires command-line access or CI/CD integration (e.g., Jenkins) to set variables dynamically.

entry.title: Re: How to Migrate Stock from IM Managed to EWM Managed S...
entry.content:
Hi Melani,

Yes, the standard Migration Cockpit (LTMC) is meant for initial stock uploads, not for transferring stock between IM- and EWM-managed storage locations in an already live system.

For stock transfers from IM to EWM-managed SLocs in S/4HANA Public Cloud, you should use stock transport orders (STOs) or posting change deliveries, depending on your process setup. This ensures that the inventory, batch, and HU data are updated across both systems while respecting EWM handling.

If HUs are involved, use delivery-based transfer with handling units via outbound delivery (VL01N equivalent via Fiori app) and EWM inbound processing.

Make sure you’ve configured the relevant warehouse integration and delivery types (e.g., NL for STO, PCD for posting changes).

Refer below

3136651 - Switching from an IM storage location to a Cloud WM managed location 

Hope that helps,
Chuma

entry.title: Re: Open Journal Entry App in a New Tab from P&L or G/L R...
entry.content:
Hello @ParkJongKyu 

You're right,in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, standard Fiori behaviour opens linked apps like Manage Journal Entries in the same tab, which can disrupt workflows when users want to keep the source report open.
Can this be changed?

SAP Fiori apps in the Public Cloud are launched within the same shell context. SAP does not offer a built-in option to force links to open in new browser tabs via app settings or Launchpad configuration.
Workarounds:

  • Right-click → “Open link in new tab” (when available).
    This works on interactive fields like Journal Entry numbers, especially in browser views.
  • Ctrl+Click or Middle-click
    In most modern browsers, Ctrl+Click (or clicking the scroll wheel) on a link opens it in a new tab works for many clickable fields in Fiori apps.
  • Use Side-by-Side apps (if applicable)
    Some apps, like Display Line Items in General Ledger, support side panels or popups, but this is limited and not user-configurable.

There’s no standard config to force new-tab behaviour in S/4HANA Public Cloud today. Best you can do is educate users to Ctrl+Click or right-click where possible.

SAP Note 2910170

With kind regards

Chuma

entry.title: GRC Tuesdays: Regulatory Compliance Trends and Outlook
entry.content:
If looming trade wars and changing trade compliance dynamics have monopolized the debate recently, it’s undeniable that there are many other regulatory requirements that companies face – and more to come in the near future.

In addition to the top risks report released at the beginning of the year (What Risks to Look Out for in 2025), I thought it might also be relevant to publish a consolidated view of the most often cited regulatory challenges for organizations. Immediate challenges, but also upcoming ones already on the radar for future enforcement.

This blog summarizes the findings of 23+ individual reports as well as interviews with executives. It is by no means perfect nor exhaustive, but I am still hoping that it will prove useful for anyone in a compliance, audit or risk management role looking at planning the way ahead.

 

Long story short

 

Cybersecurity & information protection are the major regulatory concerns for companies, closely followed by specific emerging laws for trusted and responsible Artificial Intelligence. Some may argue that trusted AI should also be part of Cybersecurity, and there are indeed areas of overlap, but since AI legislation goes beyond data protection and ventures into ethics and governance, I decided to follow the lead of reports that keep them separated.

Combined with Environmental, Social and Governance and rapidly evolving Trade Compliance, these 4 categories together already cover over 50% of compliance concerns for companies for 2025-2027.

 

Top compliance requirements for next 12-24 months

 

Altogether, the first 6 categories in the list are cited in 75% of the cases as main challenges facing organizations:

 

Cybersecurity & Information Protection

 

 

This grouping encapsulates requirements designed to safeguard information systems and data from cyber threats. Data privacy and protection and breach notification requirements are of course also in the scope here. Examples of legislations include the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) or the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) for instance.

 

Trusted/Responsible AI

 

 

As mentioned earlier in this blog, this is a separate category to Cybersecurity since it doesn’t focus exclusively on the data protection, but also includes ethical and accountability to ensure that artificial intelligence systems operate fairly and without discriminatory bias. One example here would be the EU AI Act but also the South Korea AI Framework Act.

 

Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG)

 

 

Here, requirements are meant to ensure that companies operate responsibly and sustainably. Associated standards are designed to measure a company's impact on the environment (climate change, natural resource and waste management), its social responsibilities (diversity and inclusion, protection of human rights), and its governance practices (board diversity, compensation model). The International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS), Sustainability Disclosure Standards, Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) standards or the EU Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) are good illustrations.

 

International Trade

 

 

This compliance category refers to the processes and procedures guaranteeing that goods and services imported and exported are done so in accordance with the legislative framework and this includes Export Control, Free Trade Agreements, Sanctions and Embargo obligations and more. There is no overarching regulation, but some examples here can be U.S. Export Administration Regulations (EAR), the Australia-United States Free Trade Agreement (AUSFTA), and the United Nations Security Council (UNSC) Sanctions List.

 

Financial and Operational Resiliency

 

 

This section addresses the regulatory requirements that ensure organizations, can withstand and recover from disruptions, whether they be financial or operational failures. Historically, financial services and providers of essential services have been the core focus, but compliance requirements are now expanding to many other sectors. In addition to Basel III, other examples include the UK Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) and Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) Rules, the Digital Operational Resilience Act (DORA) or the various Cybersecurity Supply Chain Risk Management (C-SCRM) legislations around the world.

 

Consumer Protection

 

 

Simply put, these are the laws and regulations designed to safeguard consumers from unfair, deceptive, or abusive practices in the marketplace. The scope is quite large, ranging from fair and equitable lending practices, to product safety standards, preventing product misrepresentation in advertising, etc. These are represented by the Consumer Rights Directive in the European Union, the requirements of the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) or Federal Trade Commission (FTC) in the United States for instance.

And there are many more of course but, as for the annual blog on risk, my intent here is to provide a baseline and then revisit each year to identify new trends instead of listing all possible obligatory mandates.

What about you, what are the top regulatory topics on your mind? I look forward to reading your thoughts and comments on this blog.

And if you are interested in learning more about SAP solutions for Governance, Risk, and Compliance, feel free to fill-in the demo request form!

entry.title: Unsafe-eval error in SAP Build Work Zone: Understanding and fixing a CSP blockage
entry.content:
While developing a SAP Fiori application in SAP Business Application Studio (BAS), I embedded an external component within a custom SAPUI5 component. The application worked perfectly in the local development environment, but once deployed into SAP Build Work Zone (BWZ), it no longer loaded as expected.

The browser console revealed an error related to the Content Security Policy (CSP), specifically due to the indirect use of eval(). This blog post summarizes my learnings about CSP and how I resolved the issue.

What is the Content Security Policy (CSP)?

CSP is a web security standard defined by the W3C and enforced by web browsers. It allows developers to control which resources (scripts, images, styles, etc.) a browser is allowed to load and execute, helping to prevent Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attacks and malicious script injections.

For example, the following directive means that only scripts from the same origin as the application are permitted.

Content-Security-Policy: script-src 'self'

Other common directives include:

  • style-src: Specifies allowed sources for stylesheets.
  • img-src: Limits where images can be loaded from.
  • connect-src: Defines allowed sources for fetching resources like APIs.
  • default-src: Specifies a fallback for all resource types if not otherwise defined.
  • frame-ancestors: Controls which sources can embed the page in an iframe.
  • … 

To learn more about CSP, refer to the official documentation on MDN: https://developer.mozilla.org/fr/docs/Web/HTTP/CSP

Problem context

The application was developed in SAP Business Application Studio and contained an external web component wrapped inside a custom SAPUI5 component.

Once deployed to SAP Build Work Zone, the interface no longer displayed at all. The browser console then showed the following error:

Unsafe-eval error (CSP Compliant)
Uncaught (in promise) EvalError:
Refused to evaluate a string as JavaScript because 'unsafe-eval' is not an allowed source of script
in the following Content Security Policy directive: "script-src * data: blob:".

Error Meaning

In CSP, dynamic functions like eval() or new Function() are blocked by default because they can introduce serious security risks. These functions allow JavaScript code to be generated and executed at runtime, which can be exploited to inject and run malicious scripts—commonly associated with XSS attacks.

To protect the application's security, dynamic code execution is blocked by default in the Content Security Policy (CSP). Allowing it would require the inclusion of the unsafe-eval directive in the CSP, but this weakens security and should be avoided in production environments.

Root cause analysis

In the external component I used, I was generating an HTML element returned as a string (this is where I messed up). This HTML was then dynamically injected into the component’s DOM. However, the external component was interpreting this string using new Function(), violating CSP rules.

This behavior went unnoticed locally in Business Application Studio because CSP policies are often disabled or applied more leniently in development environments. However, in SAP Build Work Zone, a strict CSP is enforced, blocking dynamic code evaluation like this.

Implemented solution

By consulting the official documentation of the component, I found that I could provide a JavaScript function directly instead of passing a returned string containing HTML. This approach enables the component to use a safe rendering function, avoiding reliance on blocked mechanisms like eval() or new Function().

Here is an example of the new CSP-compliant code:

const generateDropdown = (aOptions) => {
  // Create the select element
  const oSelectElement = document.createElement('select');
  oSelectElement.classList.add('myCustomSelectClass');

  // Loop through the options array and create option elements
  aOptions.forEach(oOption => {
    const oOptionElement = document.createElement('option');
    oOptionElement.value = oOption.value;
    oOptionElement.textContent = oOption.label;

    // Append the option to the select
    oSelectElement.appendChild(oOptionElement); 
  });

  return oSelectElement;
};

// Example usage with an array of options
const options = [
  { value: '1', label: 'Option 1' },
  { value: '2', label: 'Option 2' },
  { value: '3', label: 'Option 3' }
];

// Generate the dropdown dynamically
generateDropdown(options);

Lessons learned

An application that works correctly in BAS may encounter critical errors in BWZ if it does not comply with the CSP. It is essential to prioritize the use of real JavaScript functions over injecting HTML or JavaScript code as strings. Often, the documentation for external components provides alternative solutions that are CSP-compliant, and these should be carefully considered.

Conclusion

The Content Security Policy is an essential tool for securing web applications against XSS attacks and the execution of uncontrolled code. In environments like SAP Build Work Zone, CSP is strictly enforced, which can lead to unexpected errors.

By adapting our implementation to comply with CSP rules, we not only ensure the compatibility of our applications but also enhance their security. I hope this feedback will be useful to you.

entry.title: Expanding SAP Form Service by Adobe Use Cases Out of SAP ERP Context
entry.content:
This service is no stranger to most SAP customers as it is the go to service when comes to generation of interactive forms or static PDF forms based on pre-configured templates, that may be used as attachments for transactions or documents on ECC or S/4HANA.

However, with the advancement in technology over the years, I cannot stop to wonder, why are customers prefer or still using this two-decade old service even though there are other innovative solutions in the market?  I noticed another problem while researching on the service via the web, most of the blogs and SAP’s help documentation only focuses on integration with ECC or S/4HANA using ABAP coding. This sounds like a good technology that can be used outside of SAP context and have led me to my next burning question, "Did anyone try to maximise this service beyond SAP context and with the use of python?" Sadly, there is limited information on this.

Recently, I had a chance to build and demonstrate a proof of concept with this service on BTP, based on an actual use case. Finally, I got all my answers. Hence, let’s dive into the details.

Use Case:

Customer is keen on exploring BTP services to create an application for their ground staff to generate unique labels for their finished goods. These are some of their business requirements:

  1. Ground staffs can input production data into the application.
  2. Ground staffs can use the custom-built application to pull S/4HANA data and use it as part of label creation.
  3. The label follows a standard format but, it comes with a unique QR code and contents.
  4. Current label template is on SAP Form Service by Adobe, and customer has specifically instructed to leverage on existing service and template.

Solution:

Create an application using SAP Build Apps, a low-code, no-code service for frontend development and a backend service using python, that integrates with database, object repository and SAP Form Service by Adobe. The backend service will be hosted on SAP Cloud Foundry Runtime.

Technical Architecture

For this blog, I will be focusing only on using SAP Form Service by Adobe and calling the service via python with the standard APIs.

How to start?

Prerequisite: To create form templates, you will need to download and install Adobe Lifecycle Designer. There are some SAP Notes and SAP’s help documentation available online that can guide you on how to get this software. Please refer to this link for more information.

Step 1: Create a form template using Adobe Lifecycle Designer

First realisation: Adobe lifecycle designer is a business user friendly software.

The drag and drop feature within Adobe Lifecycle Designer, enables user to create form template easily. It comes with a comprehensive object library containing the most commonly used objects (components) like button, check box, etc., for a form. It also allows user to embed a QR code in the template and specify the data beneath it. All the user needs to do is to drag their desired objects into the design panel and design the form template.

Step 2: Create a data schema in xsd format

These are the XML data that I wish to insert into the form template. Let’s convert the XML data into xml data schema in xsd format.

Step 3: Bind the data with the objects in the template and export the template

User has the option to bind with a sample XML data file to visualise the final form outcome before binding it with a xml schema. Once this is done, export the template in xdp format.

Step 4: Activate SAP Form Service by Adobe via BTP cockpit. Access the UI to upload template and data schema.

You can refer to this link to learn more about the service setup on BTP cockpit.

Once the service is set up and with the appropriate roles assigned, you may access the SAP Form Service Landing page.

Step 5: Upload Form Template and Data Schema under “Template Store”

First, create a Form on SAP Form Service by Adobe. You can proceed to upload the form template (xdp file) and data schema (xsd file) into the service.

Second realisation: Python can be used to create the necessary files for PDF rendering.

Step 6: Create a backend python service

a. Use the SAP Form Service by Adobe standard API to pull the template from ADS service.

b. Generate encoded xml data encoded Base64 file using Xml.etree.Element tree library.

c. Render PDF to generate label via API

When Render PDF API (labelled as ADS - Render Requests on SAP Business Accelerator Hub [link]) is called with the necessary payload information, the response will return a Base64 encoded file of the rendered PDF. You will need to decode it and save it to your desired destination, either on local machine or into an object repository.

Output

Conclusion

As you can see from above, SAP Form Service by Adobe can be further leverage by expanding the use cases outside of ERP context, with the use of a different coding language, python. I hope that this blog can serve as a good reference for the community to maximise the usage of this service.

Happy learning and exploring!

entry.title: SAP分析云微件API介绍
entry.content:
1.什么是SAP 分析云微件API？

​​SAP 分析云（SAC）微件API 是一项强大的开发工具，允许开发者将SAP分析云故事中的图表嵌入外部应用程序。本博客将概述微件API，引导您逐步完成集成过程，并演示其如何增强应用程序的分析能力。

2.SAP 分析云微件API概览

SAP分析云微件API​​ 支持将SAP分析云可视化对象无缝嵌入外部Web应用程序、门户系统或自定义构建系统中。借助该API，您可以实现以下功能：

  1. 在Web应用程序中展示交互式图表​​
  2. 以编程方式控制内容​​（筛选、刷新、调整大小等）
  3. 通过身份验证确保应用与SAP分析云间的安全通信​​
  4. 通过将分析直接嵌入桌面及Web应用程序，达到更佳用户体验​

3.前提条件

​在开始之前，请确保您已满足以下要求：

  1. 访问SAP分析云并拥有所需权限​​。例如：用户将能够登录SAP分析云并查看故事的访问权限
  2. 想要嵌入的 ​​微件ID​​、​​故事 ID​​ 及 ​​租户 ID​​（请参考第3节了解如何获取微件ID）
  3. 具备​​JavaScript与HTML的基础知识

4.微件API功能

微件API的一些关键功能包括：

  1. ​​嵌入图表：从SAP分析云故事中集成单个或多个图表。
  2. ​​筛选与参数：以编程方式应用筛选器并变更视图​
  3. ​​上下文菜单和交互：图表将是交互式的，您可以选择数据点进行筛选并应用各种交互选项。
  4. ​​跨源通信​​：父应用程序与SAP分析云之间的安全通信。

5.使用微件API逐步集成SAP图表

①生成嵌入链接

在SAP分析云中：

  1. 打开您想要嵌入的故事的编辑模式。
  2. 导航到您想要嵌入的微件。在您的Web应用程序中使用它。（请参阅第3节以获取微件ID）

②设置Web应用程序

这里有一个简单的HTML结构，用于将SAP分析云微件集成到网页中。

③ ​​了解API结构

此API支持将SAP分析云微件无缝嵌入外部应用程序。

包括三个主要元素：​​一个命名空间​​、​​一个函数调用​​及​​一组参数（一些是必需的，一些是可选的）。

这些是API的元素：

1.命名空间：

定义了访问API组件的范围，指定的命名空间是：**sap.sac.api.widget**

2.函数调用：

调用的主函数是：**renderWidget**

3.必需参数:

要成功渲染微件，必须提供以下参数：

调用主函数为renderWidget，必选参数：

        a.容器ID（Container ID）​​

​​        指定应用程序中用于嵌入微件的HTML容器（如 <div> 元素）​

        b.租户URL（Tenant URL）​​

        SAP 分析云租户微件所在位置的基础 URL

        示例： https://sample.tenant.cloud/sap/fpa/ui

        c.SAP分析云故事ID（SAC Story ID）

         包含微件的SAP分析云故事ID，要获取故事ID

         请在SAP分析云中打开故事并从URL中提取。
         示例URL：https://sample.tenant.cloud/sap/fpa/ui/app.html#/story2&/s2/C7D03902C194A72F5F130EC083762CCA → 故事ID：C7D03902C194A72F5F130EC083762CCA

        d.微件ID

         指示要嵌入的特定微件。

          在编辑模式下，您可以在样式设置面板的通用属性中找到微件ID 。

           例如，微件ID可能是：chart_3

 在查看模式下，在微件处右键点击，选择更多选项>复制微件ID：

注：请参考SAP分析云帮助文档以编程方式获取SAP分析云故事ID和微件ID:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ANALYTICS_CLOUD/a4406994704e4af5a8559a640b496468/f0827b68d24548448060c... 

4.可选参数

以下参数在API中是可选的：

启用和禁用微件交互：您可以通过传递这些可选参数来禁用用户的微件交互。

注意：默认情况下，所有这些参数都设置为true。

       1.enableInteraction：true/false

使用此属性，您可以启用或禁用微件中的交互。

       2.enableMenu: true/false

使用此属性，您可以启用或禁用微件的上下文菜单。

       3.showHeader: true/false

使用此属性，您可以启用或禁用微件的页眉。

       4.showFooter: true/false

     使用此属性，您可以启用或禁用微件的页脚。

示例：

6.认证与安全注意事项

为了安全通信，SAP分析云需要身份验证。根据您的设置：

  • 如果您的用户已经在外部应用程序中通过了身份验证，使用基于SAML的SSO

请确保您的SAP分析云实例和外部应用程序配置为允许跨源资源共享（CORS），以实现无缝数据交换。

7.应用场景：客户门户中的嵌入式分析

试想以下方案：某银行通过客户门户为用户提供对其支出模式的实时洞察。通过使用微件 API将SAP分析云的图表嵌入门户，银行可以：

  1. 显示根据每个用户的交易定制的交互式仪表板。
  2. 允许用户直接从门户按日期或类别筛选数据。
  3. 发生新交易时自动刷新图表。

这种集成增强了用户体验，并使客户能够获得可操作的洞察。

参考应用：
我们已发布基于SAP分析云微件API开发的SAP Analytics Cloud PowerPoint插件，现在您可以将SAP分析云故事微件以图像形式嵌入PPT，该图像会随数据更新而动态刷新。

 

8.示例：使用微件API集成SAP分析云微件的网页示例

  1. 安装Visual Studio Code
    下载链接：https://code.visualstudio.com/
  2. 安装"Live Server"扩展

3.在Visual Studio Code应用程序中打开示例HTML代码文件

4.在初始化displayWidget函数时添加SAP分析云租户详细信息。

5.在displayWidget函数中添加微件ID

6.点击"Go Live"按钮启动服务器，将在浏览器中运行应用程序。

7.此外，您还可以传递过滤器和变量

sap.sac.api.widget.renderWidget(

    containerId,

    tenant,

    storyId,

    widgetId,

    {

        filters: [

            {

                model: "sap.epm.Planned_Events_Sample:Planned_Events_Sample",

                values: [{

                    dimension: "Date_5x2a25042z",

                    value: ["026b7dcf2350c824559b8cc00612de", "05aff8031d1c560fa0ea6a675cc894", "53a4b48d6e946faa59c35643d775c9"],

                    operation: 'in'

                }]

            },      

            {

                model: "sap.epm.Planned_Events_Sample:Planned_Events_Sample",

                values: [{

                    dimension: "Event_672z323k1f",

                    value: [""],

                    operation: 'notIn'

                }]

            },

            {

                model: "sap.epm.Planned_Events_Sample:Planned_Events_Sample",

                values: [{

                    dimension: "Date_5x2a25042z",

                    value: ["026b7dcf2350c824559b8cc00612de"],

                    operation: 'notIn'

                }]

            }

        ]

    },

    updatedOptions

);

9.导出图表

一旦微件成功嵌入应用程序后，微件 API 将提供可选参数，用于在图表渲染完成后导出。该API的结构由命名空间、函数调用及若干参数（部分为可选）组成。

API概览

1.命名空间:

命名空间为：sap.sac.api.widget

2.函数调用:

用于导出的函数：export

3.参数 ：

containerId（必填）该参数为字符串类型，表示嵌入微件的 HTML 容器 ID。示例​​：若容器 ID 为 widget1，则应传递值 "widget1"。​​​​format(可选)指定导出格式的字符串。默认值是“svg”，这是目前唯一支持的格式。Options（可选）您可以使用options对象进一步配置导出，包括：Include用于控制是否显示图表的​​标题​​、​​副标题​​和​​页脚​​的对象，每个属性（标题、副标题、页脚）都是boolean。默认值：false。Size为导出的SVG设置自定义尺寸的对象。属性：高度和宽度（均为数字）默认值：当前容器的尺寸

示例：

const options = {

  include: {

    title: true,

    subtitle: true,

    footer: true

  },

  size: {

    height: 300,

    width: 300

  }

}

await sap.sac.api.widget.export("widget1", "svg", options)

10.故障排查提示

  1. 图表未加载？确保 SAP分析云租户 URL 正确且可从 Web 应用程序访问。
  2. CORS 错误？验证 SAP分析云实例是否允许来自您应用程序域的 CORS。
  3. 认证问题？确认有效的身份验证或 SSO 设置已正确配置。

11.更多阅读与资源

启用Iframe嵌入功能

SAP 微件API 开发文档

 原文链接: Introducing SAP Analytics cloud Widget API - SAP Community

 原作者： Ashok_R

entry.title: Mastering the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Release Readiness Check: What Every Customer Should Know
entry.content:
As SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition continues to evolve at a rapid pace, staying current with biannual upgrades isn’t just important—it’s essential. Each release brings powerful innovations, new capabilities, and improvements across lines of business. But with every upgrade comes an opportunity—and a responsibility—for customers to prepare their systems, integrations, and teams to ensure a smooth transition.

That’s exactly where the Release Readiness Check comes in. Delivered as part of the SAP Preferred Success offering, this proactive report empowers customers to detect potential risks in their landscape before they surface in production. From open transports and deprecated objects to test automation gaps and process vulnerabilities, the report provides clear, usage-based insights that help you act early—and upgrade confidently.

Customers entitled to the Preferred Success program in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition can request this report directly from their Customer Success Manager (CSM). While the report can be requested at any point in time, it’s most effective when reviewed well before an upgrade window—giving your team enough lead time to interpret the insights and act accordingly. For example, if your upgrade is scheduled for August, it’s ideal to request this service in May, June, or early July.

In this blog, we’ll break down the key sections of the Release Readiness Check report, explain what each tile means, and share how you can leverage these insights to make your upgrade cycle more predictable, stable, and risk-free.

 

1.  Open CBC Transports – Why It Deserves Your Attention

This tile is especially relevant if you’re operating in a 3-system landscape (3SL) for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

Let’s add some background: With the 3-system landscape for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, SAP introduced content decoupling during update and upgrade cycles. This means that while the software code is updated first, content is delivered separately through SAP Central Business Configuration (CBC) and deployed automatically to the development system. You, as the customer, could then transport it to the test and production systems using available apps.

This feature lets you to temporarily opt out of content updates or upgrades. Consequently, transport requests may remain in your 3-system landscape for too long and could result in a situation where it can't be imported by you, violating the "downward compatibility rule"

The downward compatibility rule, also known as the n+1 rule, allows you to import content from the current (n) or previous (n-1) release, but not older (n-2) releases.

Here, "n" represents the release number, such as 2502. "n+1" refers to the next release, such as 2508. Importing content from older releases (n-2) isn't possible without SAP support.

You may be flagged in the report if:

  • You released an SAP CBC transport request for the n-1 release and did not import the transport into the test system
  • You released an SAP CBC transport request for the n-1 release and imported the transport into the test system. However, you did not forward it to the production system, even though the production system exists.
  • You released an SAP CBC transport request for the n-1 release and imported the transport into the test system. However, you did not yet forward it to the production system, because the production system was not yet provisioned

For you the action - Identify the transports at risk and import them before it’s too late.”

Find some more details on the same here : https://me.sap.com/notes/003610869

 

2. Deprecated Objects – Plan Your Cleanup Early

This tile flags SAP-deprecated objects still in use in your production system. These might include apps, APIs, CDS views, or other entities that SAP has announced as obsolete.

What to Do

Use the Release Assessment and Scope Dependency (RASD) tool (https://sap.com/rasd)  to:

  • Review the deprecated items flagged.
  • Find their successors.
  • Check the deprecation timeline and announcement details.
  • Use the "Successor Companion" tab in RASD for migration and remediation guidance.

This is your chance to avoid broken links post-upgrade and streamline your business architecture.

 

3. Process Automation Bots – Test Before It Breaks

If you’re using SAP Process Automation bots, some may rely on screen scraping or UI recording. When UI layouts or field IDs change with an upgrade, such bots can fail.

Recommended Action

  • Check for the bots in your Business Technology Platform environment
  • Investigate whether a standard API exists and switch to that if possible.
  • Ensure all flagged bots are included in your regression testing scope before the upgrade hits production.

Don’t wait for a post-upgrade surprise—validate your bots early.

 

4. Test Automation Tool – Your Regression Testing Companion

SAP strongly recommends running regression tests after every release to proactively catch issues before they impact your production environment. The Test Automation Tool plays a vital role in this process, allowing you to validate your critical business processes efficiently and consistently. As part of the Post-Upgrade Test service, automated tests can be initiated either by SAP or by you as the customer, provided you have granted consent within your SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition quality or test tenant.

These tests can include standard SAP-delivered test scripts as well as custom automates that reflect your unique business processes. The Release Readiness Check helps you assess two key areas: whether you have opted into this post-upgrade testing service, and your actual usage of the tool over the past six months. This insight is crucial, especially if your regression testing efforts have been minimal or inconsistent.

Neglecting regression testing puts you at risk of post-upgrade disruptions, such as broken processes, failed integrations, or compliance gaps. If you’re unsure how to set up the tool or want to expand your testing scope, SAP Preferred Success offers expert guidance on strategy, setup, and execution. Reach out to your Customer Success Manager to make test automation a core part of your upgrade readiness journey.

 

Wrapping It All Up

The Release Readiness Check is more than a technical report—it’s your early warning system to ensure your S/4HANA Cloud landscape is stable, supported, and ready for what’s next.

Key Takeaways:

  • Open CBC Transports must be proactively managed to avoid version-locks.
  • Deprecated objects should be cleaned up to prevent process failures.
  • Process automation bots should be revalidated to avoid runtime errors.
  • Test automation is not optional—automate early, test smartly.

Let’s work together to make every upgrade a moment of opportunity, not anxiety.

If you’ve received the report and need help interpreting any of the tiles, reach out to your SAP Customer Success Manager. We're here to help make your journey risk-free, future-ready, and fully supported.
